decomposition_notes: Multi-goal split along the three orthogonal seams from the Sketch section. G1 (events + queue) emits notification_requested into Oban; G2 (preferences UI) lets users opt in to channels and configure DND; G3 (email rendering + dispatch) consumes the queue and emails out. G1 and G2 are independent and can be claimed in any order. G3 depends on G1 — claim G1 first, let its queue contract land in main, then claim G3. G2 is fully parallel.

goals.title: Wire notification event detection and Oban queue
goals.why: Approval lag p50 is 28h because time-sensitive events only surface in-app; events must reach an out-of-band channel via a deduping queue.
goals.what: Emit notification_requested from approval lifecycle / comment mentions / dependency-unblocked, and land them on an Oban queue keyed by recipient + event class so a recipient never gets the same event twice.
goals.description: Independent of UI and email work; produces the queue contract both downstream goals depend on.
goals.acceptance_criteria:
Approval lifecycle emits notification_requested when an approval is owed
Comment mentions emit notification_requested at create time
Dependency-unblocked emits notification_requested
Oban queue dedupes by (recipient_id, event_class)
pitfalls: Do not introduce a new background-job library; reuse the existing Oban setup

tasks.title: Define notification_requested event shape and module
tasks.description: Add MyApp.Notifications.Event struct with recipient_id, event_class, payload, occurred_at. Used as the queue input contract.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Event struct defined
Unit test asserts struct construction + required fields

unit_tests: Struct builds with recipient_id, event_class, payload, occurred_at set
Missing required field (recipient_id or event_class) is rejected or flagged
occurred_at defaults to a UTC DateTime when not supplied
edge_cases: payload is an empty map
event_class is an unknown/unsupported atom or string
occurred_at supplied as a naive vs UTC datetime

security_considerations: recipient_id must reference a real user so downstream dispatch cannot leak to an arbitrary id
payload must not carry secrets or full PII blobs that will later be serialized into an Oban args column
event_class should be a bounded/known set, not free-form user input, to avoid poisoning the dedupe key
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use defstruct with @enforce_keys for the required fields (recipient_id, event_class)
Mirror existing struct/typespec conventions in lib/app/notifications/ and other domain structs
Keep the struct a pure data contract with no Repo or side-effect calls
Add a @type t :: %__MODULE__{} typespec
pitfalls: Do not embed the full user record in payload — carry only ids the renderer can re-load
Do not use String.to_atom/1 on incoming event_class values
Do not add persistence or enqueue logic to this struct module; it is only the contract

tasks.title: Add :notifications Oban queue config
tasks.description: Register the new :notifications queue in the existing Oban config with a sensible concurrency limit.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Queue is supervised
Concurrency limit set to a value that respects SMTP relay limits

unit_tests: Oban config includes a :notifications queue key with an integer concurrency limit
integration_tests: Application boots with the queue supervised and Oban.check_queue/1 reports :notifications running
manual_tests: Confirm the queue appears in the Oban dashboard / supervision tree after boot
edge_cases: Concurrency set too high relative to SMTP relay rate limits
Test/dev environments where Oban is in :inline or testing mode
Queue name collision with an existing configured queue
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Config change verified via a boot/supervision assertion; no unit coverage target

security_considerations: Concurrency must respect SMTP relay limits so a burst cannot get the sending domain rate-limited or blacklisted
Do not weaken global Oban settings (pruning, retries) that could retain PII-bearing job args longer than needed
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Add the queue to the existing Oban queues keyword list in config/config.exs — do not start a second Oban instance
Respect per-environment overrides in config/test.exs (testing: :inline) and config/runtime.exs
Match the concurrency-value style already used for the app's other queues
pitfalls: Do not introduce a new background-job library; reuse the existing Oban setup
Do not hardcode a high concurrency that ignores the SMTP relay's rate ceiling
Do not forget the test env so async tests don't accidentally execute jobs inline

tasks.title: Emit notification_requested from approval lifecycle
tasks.description: Hook the existing approval-state-transition path so an 'approval owed to X' transition emits a notification_requested event to the queue.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Approval owed transition emits event
Unit test asserts an event is enqueued when the approval is created

unit_tests: Approval-owed transition enqueues exactly one notification_requested job
Non-owed / non-relevant transitions enqueue nothing
Enqueued job carries the correct recipient_id and event_class :approval_owed
integration_tests: Creating an approval that owes a user results in a queued Oban job for that user
edge_cases: Approval owed to the actor themselves
Approval reassigned then re-owed (no duplicate emission beyond dedupe intent)
Transition inside a failing DB transaction should not leave an orphan job
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the emit hook in lib/app/approvals/lifecycle.ex

security_considerations: recipient_id must be the user genuinely owed the approval — never leak an approval event to an unrelated user
Do not place approval subject content or PII in the event payload; carry the approval id and re-authorize at render time
Ensure tenancy/scope of the approval is preserved so cross-tenant approvals never notify the wrong recipient
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Hook the existing approval state-transition path rather than adding a parallel one
Enqueue via the notifications queue module (Tasks.Positioning-style single entry point), not a raw Oban.insert scattered in the lifecycle
Emit inside the same Ecto.Multi/transaction as the transition so the event is consistent with the state change
Go through the context/queue module, never call Repo.insert on the job directly from lifecycle logic
pitfalls: Do not emit on every approval save — only on the owed transition
Do not emit before the transition commits (avoid notifying about a rolled-back approval)
Do not duplicate-emit when the lifecycle is re-run idempotently

tasks.title: Emit notification_requested from comment mentions
tasks.description: On comment create, scan for @mentions and emit one notification_requested event per mentioned user.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Mention scanning emits one event per unique user
Unit test covers single mention, multi-mention, self-mention (suppressed)

unit_tests: Single @mention emits one event for that user
Multiple distinct @mentions emit one event each, deduped per unique user
Self-mention is suppressed (no event for the comment author)
integration_tests: Creating a comment with mentions enqueues one job per mentioned user
edge_cases: Same user mentioned twice in one comment collapses to one event
@mention of a non-existent or deactivated username
Mention-like text inside a code block or escaped @ should not fire
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the mention scanner + emitter in lib/app/comments/mentions.ex

security_considerations: Resolve mentions only to users who can actually view the comment/thread — do not notify users who lack access to the parent resource (tenancy scoping)
Do not copy raw comment body into the event payload; keep PII/user text out of Oban args and pass the comment id
Guard against mention-spam: a single comment mentioning many users could enqueue a flood of jobs
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Scan mentions in the comment context on create, not in the LiveView
Reuse the existing user-lookup/resolution helper rather than a new regex-to-user path where one exists
Emit through the shared notifications queue entry point, one Event per unique recipient
Filter recipients by visibility/scope before emitting
pitfalls: Do not emit a duplicate event when the same user is mentioned multiple times
Do not notify the comment author for self-mentions
Do not notify users who cannot see the thread the comment belongs to

tasks.title: Emit notification_requested when a dependency is unblocked
tasks.description: When a task's last blocking dependency completes, emit notification_requested for the task's owner.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Dependency-unblock transition emits event
Unit test covers single-dep and chain-of-deps cases

unit_tests: Completing the last blocking dependency emits one event for the task owner
Completing a non-final dependency (task still blocked) emits nothing
Chain of dependencies emits only when the task becomes fully unblocked
integration_tests: Completing a task that unblocks a dependent enqueues a job for the dependent's owner
edge_cases: Task with multiple simultaneous blockers completing together
Task with no owner assigned
Cyclic or already-unblocked dependency should not double-emit
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the emit hook in lib/app/tasks/dependencies.ex

security_considerations: recipient_id must be the dependent task's owner within the same board/tenant — never notify across tenants
Payload should carry the unblocked task id, not the full task record, to keep PII out of the job args
Ensure only genuinely unblocked transitions emit so the queue cannot be flooded by repeated recomputation
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Emit from the existing dependency-resolution path in lib/app/tasks/dependencies.ex, not from unrelated task-update code
Determine unblocked state via the existing blocking-count logic rather than reimplementing it
Enqueue through the shared notifications queue entry point inside the same transaction as the dependency completion
Scope the owner lookup through the task's board/tenant
pitfalls: Do not emit while the task still has other open blockers
Do not emit on every dependency edge change — only on the last-blocker-cleared transition
Do not emit when the task has no owner to notify

tasks.title: Implement queue dedupe by (recipient_id, event_class)
tasks.description: Before persisting an enqueued event, look up existing pending events for the same recipient + event class and collapse the payload into the existing job.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Duplicate events within a window collapse
Unit test asserts two enqueues of the same recipient+class produce one job

unit_tests: Two enqueues of the same recipient_id + event_class produce a single job
Different event_class for the same recipient produces separate jobs
The second enqueue collapses its payload into the existing pending job
integration_tests: Rapid burst of identical events within the window yields one Oban job in the DB
edge_cases: Concurrent enqueues racing to create the same key (rely on unique index / Oban uniqueness)
An already-executing/completed job for the key should not block a new distinct notification
Payload merge when the two events carry conflicting fields
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the dedupe logic in lib/app/notifications/queue.ex

security_considerations: event_class and recipient_id form the dedupe key — untrusted/unbounded event_class values could poison or evade dedupe, so keep the key values bounded
Collapsing payloads must not merge one recipient's data into another's job (strict key equality on recipient_id)
Ensure the collapse cannot be abused to suppress a legitimately distinct notification
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Prefer Oban's built-in unique job option (unique: [keys, period, states]) over a hand-rolled SELECT-then-INSERT where possible
If collapsing manually, do the lookup+update inside a transaction with a DB unique constraint as the race backstop
Keep all dedupe logic in the queue context module, not in the callers that emit events
Mirror existing Oban worker/uniqueness conventions in lib/app/workers or lib/app/notifications
pitfalls: Do not implement dedupe with a naive read-then-write that races under concurrency without a unique constraint
Do not dedupe across different event_class or different recipient_id
Do not collapse into jobs that are already executing/completed, only pending ones

goals.title: Build user notification preferences UI
goals.why: Users must explicitly opt in to channels (CAN-SPAM + GDPR); without preferences, nothing can be sent.
goals.what: Add a preferences LiveView, the user_notification_preferences schema, per-event-class opt-in toggles, per-user DND window, and email-address override.
goals.description: Independent of G1 and G3. Can be claimed in parallel with either.
goals.acceptance_criteria:
Preferences LiveView lets users opt in per event class
DND window persisted per user
Email-address override defaults to account email
Unsubscribe link in every notification routes back to this UI
pitfalls: Do not send any email to a user who has not explicitly opted in

tasks.title: Add user_notification_preferences schema and migration
tasks.description: Schema fields: user_id, channel (string), event_class (string), enabled (bool), dnd_start, dnd_end, email_override.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Schema + migration land
Default dnd window is 22:00-08:00 in the user's TZ

unit_tests: Changeset requires user_id, channel, event_class
Default DND window is 22:00-08:00 in the user's timezone
enabled defaults to false
integration_tests: mix ecto.migrate creates the table with the expected columns and constraints
edge_cases: Duplicate (user_id, channel, event_class) row rejected by unique index
email_override left nil falls back to account email at read time
dnd_start equal to dnd_end

security_considerations: Add a not-null user_id FK with on_delete so preferences cannot outlive or reference a deleted user
email_override is user-controlled PII/routing data — it must be validated as an email and scoped to the owning user only
Unique index on (user_id, channel, event_class) prevents duplicate/conflicting opt-in rows
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Follow existing Ecto schema + changeset conventions in lib/app/accounts/
Use belongs_to :user with a matching FK in the migration and an index on user_id
Default enabled to false to honor opt-in-only sending
Validate email_override with the same email-format helper used elsewhere in Accounts
pitfalls: Do not default enabled to true — sending must be strictly opt-in
Do not store a plain string for channel/event_class without validating against the known set
Do not omit the unique constraint, or duplicate preference rows will make channel_enabled? ambiguous

tasks.title: Add MyApp.Accounts.NotificationPreferences context
tasks.description: Context functions: get_preferences/1, update_preferences/2, channel_enabled?/3, dnd_active?/2. The dispatcher (G3) calls into this to decide whether to send.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
All four functions exist and have tests
channel_enabled? defaults to false for unrecognized channels

unit_tests: get_preferences/1 returns the user's rows
update_preferences/2 persists changes atomically
channel_enabled?/3 returns false for an unrecognized channel
dnd_active?/2 returns true inside a window that crosses midnight
integration_tests: Dispatcher-style read path: channel_enabled? + dnd_active? together decide send vs skip
edge_cases: User with no preference rows (all defaults)
DND window spanning midnight (22:00-08:00) evaluated at 23:00 and 07:00
channel_enabled? for a channel the user has never toggled

security_considerations: All functions must scope by user_id so one user can never read or mutate another user's preferences
channel_enabled? must fail closed (false) for unknown channels so ambiguity never results in an unwanted send
update_preferences/2 must reject attempts to set preferences for a user other than the scoped one
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Keep all Ecto queries in this context module — never in the LiveView
Accept current_scope.user (or user_id) as the first argument and filter every query by it
Return {:ok, _}/{:error, changeset} tuples from update_preferences/2
Mirror the query/CRUD conventions of the existing Accounts context
pitfalls: Do not let channel_enabled? default to true for unrecognized channels
Do not query without scoping to the owning user
Do not evaluate the DND window with a naive start<end comparison — it must handle midnight-crossing ranges

tasks.title: Scaffold preferences LiveView mount and basic form
tasks.description: Mount loads the user's preferences via the context; render shows a per-event-class table with opt-in checkboxes.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Route /settings/notifications maps to the LiveView
Form renders one row per event class
Saving updates the database atomically

unit_tests: Changeset/form assigns build one row per event class
integration_tests: GET /settings/notifications routes to the LiveView and renders a row per event class
Toggling a checkbox and saving updates the database
Unauthenticated request is redirected to login
manual_tests: Toggle several channels, save, reload, confirm state persisted
edge_cases: User with no existing preference rows renders all-default (off) form
Save with no changes is a no-op
Concurrent save from two tabs

security_considerations: Route must be under require_authenticated_user; load and save only current_scope.user's preferences
Ignore any user_id submitted in form params — always derive the user from the session scope (prevent IDOR)
Saving must be transactional so a partial submit cannot leave inconsistent opt-in state
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Begin the template with <Layouts.app flash={@flash} current_scope={@current_scope} ...>
Place the route in the existing live_session :require_authenticated_user block with on_mount authentication
Load and persist through MyApp.Accounts.NotificationPreferences — no Repo calls in the LiveView
Use the imported <.input> / <.form> core components for the checkbox rows
pitfalls: Do not call Repo directly from the LiveView
Do not trust a user_id from form params — use current_scope.user
Do not place the route in an unauthenticated live_session

tasks.title: Add DND time-window controls to preferences form
tasks.description: Add two time-picker inputs (start, end) for the user's DND window; persist on save.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Two time inputs render
Crossing midnight is supported (e.g., 22:00-08:00)
DND window persists across reloads

verification_steps.step_text: Set DND window 22:00-08:00 and confirm dnd_active? returns true at 23:00

unit_tests: Saving start/end persists dnd_start and dnd_end
A midnight-crossing window (22:00-08:00) is accepted and stored
integration_tests: Set DND 22:00-08:00, reload the LiveView, inputs show the persisted window
dnd_active? returns true at 23:00 for a saved 22:00-08:00 window
manual_tests: Set DND window 22:00-08:00 and confirm dnd_active? returns true at 23:00
edge_cases: Window crossing midnight
start == end (zero-length or all-day interpretation)
Invalid / empty time input
testing_strategy.coverage_target: DND control handling in PreferencesLive covered; dnd_active? logic covered in context tests

security_considerations: DND window is per-user data — persist only against current_scope.user
Validate time inputs server-side; do not trust client-formatted values that could break dnd_active? evaluation
DND is a delivery control, not a security boundary — ensure it delays, never silently drops, a required notification unless the channel is also opted out
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use the imported <.input type="time"> core component for the two pickers
Persist through the NotificationPreferences context, not the LiveView
Store times in a form the context's midnight-aware dnd_active? already understands
Mirror the existing form save/validate handle_event structure in the same LiveView
pitfalls: Do not assume start < end — a 22:00-08:00 window crosses midnight
Do not compare times without accounting for the user's timezone
Do not let DND permanently drop notifications; it should delay delivery

tasks.title: Add email-address override input + unsubscribe-link route
tasks.description: Email override input defaults to the account email; unsubscribe link uses a signed token route that opens the preferences page with the relevant channel toggle pre-focused.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Email override saves and is used by the dispatcher in G3
Unsubscribe route validates the signed token before showing the form

unit_tests: Email override defaults to the account email when blank
Saved override is returned for the dispatcher to use
Signed unsubscribe token encodes user + channel and round-trips
integration_tests: Unsubscribe route with a valid signed token opens the preferences form with the channel pre-focused
Unsubscribe route with a tampered/expired token is rejected
manual_tests: Click an unsubscribe link and confirm the correct channel toggle is focused
edge_cases: Override set to a malformed email
Token for a channel the user no longer has
Expired token
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for UnsubscribeController and email-override save path

security_considerations: Unsubscribe token must be a signed (Phoenix.Token) token bound to user_id + channel with expiry — never a raw or guessable id, to prevent unsubscribe-token forgery/tampering
email_override is a routing target: validate it as a real email and consider a verification step so a user cannot redirect notifications to an address they don't control
Unsubscribe should be a scoped, low-risk action (single channel opt-out), not a route that exposes or mutates other users' preferences
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Sign and verify tokens with Phoenix.Token (or the app's existing signed-token helper), with a max_age
Validate the token in the controller before rendering or mutating anything
Persist the override through the NotificationPreferences context
Use the existing email-format validation helper in Accounts for the override input
pitfalls: Do not build the unsubscribe URL from a plain user id — it must be a signed, expiring token
Do not let the unsubscribe route mutate preferences for a user other than the token's subject
Do not skip email-format validation on the override, or the dispatcher may send to an invalid/hostile address

goals.title: Render notification emails and dispatch via Oban worker
goals.why: Once events land on the queue (G1) and users have opted in (G2), the dispatcher reads the queue and emits actual emails.
goals.what: Build the email-rendering pipeline (shared shell + per-event-class templates), wire the Oban worker that drains the :notifications queue, and add the per-message unsubscribe footer.
goals.description: Depends on G1 (the queue contract). Claim G1 first; this goal becomes claimable once G1's queue + dedupe land in main.
goals.acceptance_criteria:
Three event-class templates render (approval, mention, dependency-unblock)
Worker drains the queue and dispatches via the existing Mailer
Unsubscribe footer present in every email
Worker respects DND windows and channel opt-in from G2's preferences context
pitfalls: Do not bypass the existing audit-log infrastructure — every notification dispatch is logged
Do not send to a user whose preferences disable the channel

tasks.title: Define the email shell template (header, footer, unsubscribe link)
tasks.description: Shared HTML+text shell that wraps each event-class template. Includes the CAN-SPAM-compliant unsubscribe link with a signed token.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Shell renders for both HTML and plain-text variants
Unsubscribe link includes a signed user+channel token

unit_tests: Shell renders an HTML variant
Shell renders a plain-text variant
Rendered footer contains a signed user+channel unsubscribe link
integration_tests: An event-class body wrapped in the shell produces a complete HTML + text email
manual_tests: Preview the rendered shell in a browser / mail client for both variants
edge_cases: Missing/nil inner body
Very long unsubscribe URL wrapping in plain text
Recipient with no display name

security_considerations: Unsubscribe link must embed a signed, expiring user+channel token, never a raw id (prevents unsubscribe-token forgery)
HTML variant must escape any dynamic values interpolated into the shell to prevent HTML/link injection
Do not leak internal ids or PII beyond what the footer legitimately needs (CAN-SPAM requires a physical address / unsubscribe, not user data)
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Render through a single EmailRenderer entry point that composes shell + body for both html and text
Use Phoenix.Token for the unsubscribe token, matching G2's unsubscribe route
Keep templates under lib/app_web/templates/notifications/ following existing template conventions
Provide both .html and .text variants so the Mailer can send multipart
pitfalls: Do not hardcode an unsubscribe URL with a raw user id
Do not build only an HTML variant — a plain-text part is required
Do not interpolate unescaped dynamic content into the HTML shell

tasks.title: Render approval-owed email template
tasks.description: Per-event-class template for the approval flow. Plain text first; minimal HTML wrapper around it.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Template renders with the approval payload
Unit test asserts the rendered subject and body match the expected snapshot

unit_tests: Template renders with a valid approval payload
Rendered subject matches the expected snapshot
Rendered body matches the expected snapshot
integration_tests: Approval-owed body wrapped in the shell yields a complete email
manual_tests: Preview the approval email for both text and HTML
edge_cases: Approval with a very long title
Recipient with no display name
Payload missing an optional field
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the approval_owed template rendering path

security_considerations: Re-load approval details from the payload id and authorize that the recipient may see them — do not render approval content the user isn't entitled to
Escape any user-supplied approval title/description in the HTML variant to prevent injection
Include only necessary approval context; avoid embedding sensitive approval internals or other users' PII
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Wrap the body in the shared shell from the shell task rather than duplicating header/footer
Write plain text first, then a minimal HTML wrapper, per the task description
Store templates under lib/app_web/templates/notifications/ (approval_owed.text/html.eex)
Use snapshot-style assertions consistent with other rendering tests
pitfalls: Do not re-implement the header/footer — reuse the shell
Do not render unescaped approval text in the HTML body
Do not include approval fields the recipient is not authorized to see

tasks.title: Render mention email template
tasks.description: Mention notification template; shows the comment excerpt + a deep link.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Template renders
Unit test covers escaping of user-supplied comment text

unit_tests: Template renders with a mention payload including a comment excerpt and deep link
User-supplied comment text is HTML-escaped in the HTML variant
Deep link points at the correct comment/thread
integration_tests: Mention body wrapped in the shell yields a complete email
manual_tests: Preview a mention email containing markup-like comment text
edge_cases: Comment text containing <script> / HTML tags
Very long comment excerpt (truncation)
Comment deleted between emit and render
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the mention template rendering path, with explicit escaping coverage

security_considerations: Comment text is untrusted user input — it MUST be HTML-escaped in the HTML variant to prevent stored-XSS-via-email / HTML injection
Authorize that the recipient may view the comment/thread before rendering the excerpt (tenancy scoping)
Truncate the excerpt so a hostile giant comment cannot bloat the email or leak the whole thread
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Reuse the shared shell for header/footer/unsubscribe
Use the framework's HTML-escaping (EEx <%= %> auto-escaping / Phoenix.HTML) — never raw/1 on comment text
Re-load the comment via id from the payload rather than trusting inlined text
Keep templates under lib/app_web/templates/notifications/
pitfalls: Do not render comment text with raw/1 or otherwise bypass escaping
Do not include comment content the recipient cannot access
Do not embed the full comment body — use a bounded excerpt plus a deep link

tasks.title: Render dependency-unblocked email template
tasks.description: Per-event-class template for the dependency-unblock flow.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Template renders
Unit test asserts the rendered body names the unblocked task

unit_tests: Template renders with a dependency-unblock payload
Rendered body names the unblocked task
Deep link resolves to the unblocked task
integration_tests: Dependency-unblocked body wrapped in the shell yields a complete email
edge_cases: Task title with special/HTML characters
Task renamed or deleted between emit and render
Recipient no longer has access to the task
testing_strategy.coverage_target: 100% for the dependency_unblocked template rendering path

security_considerations: Authorize that the recipient may view the unblocked task before naming it in the email (tenancy scoping)
Escape the task title in any HTML variant to prevent injection
Re-load the task from the payload id rather than trusting inlined content that may be stale
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Reuse the shared shell for header/footer/unsubscribe
Write the plain-text variant per the task (text.eex is the primary body)
Re-load the unblocked task by id from the payload to name it accurately
Follow existing notification template conventions and snapshot-style tests
pitfalls: Do not re-implement the shell header/footer
Do not name or link a task the recipient cannot access
Do not render a stale task title from the payload without re-loading current state

tasks.title: Implement DispatchWorker that drains :notifications and respects preferences
tasks.description: Oban worker that pulls one job at a time, checks MyApp.Accounts.NotificationPreferences for channel_enabled? and dnd_active?, renders the appropriate template, and dispatches via the existing Mailer.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Worker honors channel opt-in (skips if disabled)
Worker honors DND window (delays the job if active)
Audit-log entry written for every dispatch
Unit tests cover the opt-out, DND-active, and happy-path branches

unit_tests: Happy path: enabled channel, outside DND, renders and calls Mailer once
Opt-out branch: channel disabled skips send and writes no email
DND-active branch: job is snoozed/delayed rather than sent
Audit-log entry is written for every dispatch
integration_tests: Job on the :notifications queue is drained end-to-end and an email is delivered (Swoosh test adapter)
manual_tests: Enqueue a real event and observe delivery honoring opt-in and DND
edge_cases: User has no preference row (defaults to opt-out)
email_override present — send routes to the override address
Mailer failure triggers a retry without duplicate audit rows
DND window crossing midnight while job executes

security_considerations: Must fail closed: send only when channel_enabled? is true — never dispatch to a user who has not opted in
Resolve the recipient address via the user's validated email_override/account email; do not send to an address derived from untrusted job args
Every dispatch must be written to the existing audit log for traceability (who was emailed, which event) without logging full PII payloads
Guard against Oban job poisoning: validate/normalize job args and cap retries so a malformed job cannot loop or crash the queue
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Mirror the existing Oban worker pattern (use Oban.Worker, implement perform/1) in lib/app/notifications/workers
Check MyApp.Accounts.NotificationPreferences.channel_enabled?/3 and dnd_active?/2 before rendering
Snooze via {:snooze, seconds} (or scheduled_at) for DND rather than dropping the job
Render through MyApp.Notifications.EmailRenderer and send via the existing Mailer
Write the audit entry through the existing audit-log infrastructure, not a new logger
pitfalls: Do not bypass the existing audit-log infrastructure — every notification dispatch is logged
Do not send to a user whose preferences disable the channel
Do not drop the job when DND is active — snooze/delay it instead
Do not write the audit entry before a successful send in a way that double-logs on Oban retry
